title: How the care crisis is making old age a nightmare
author: Polly Toynbee
standfirst: With no money and increasing demand from a growing elderly population, the NHS and social care services are caught in a vicious bind. Both desperately need more funding and yet find themselves in cut-throat competition
body_text: At 9am the 1,000 beds at Addenbrooke’s hospital in Cambridge were full. Senior nurses gathered together to spin through the state of play. “It’s not a fabulous bed state,” Julie Clayton, the hospital’s operations centre manager, said as she collated the news from each ward, marking the numbers of potential empty beds up on a board on the wall. That was an enormous understatement, but the atmosphere was calmly professional as they faced the start to a pretty typical day. Addenbrooke’s is a top-flight university teaching hospital, internationally famous for state-of-the-art specialisms, notably organ transplants, paediatrics, genetics and neuroscience, for which it takes referrals from a great swath of the country, stretching from the Wash to north London. One of the largest hospitals in the country, with a staff of 8,395, it’s also the district general hospital for routine cases for Cambridgeshire – and it’s the major trauma centre for all of east England, too. The Care Quality Commission (CQC), the independent body that monitors health and social care services in England, gave it a top ranking – five green ticks – on its most recent inspection in May last year. There is no better place to witness the impossible pressure building up inside the NHS; if this is among the country’s best hospitals, one hesitates to imagine the stress on the worst. Clayton’s team were facing a “significant critical internal incident”: not an accident or an emergency, just their jargon for no spare beds. “That means we can’t cope with what’s coming in,” she said. “All non–urgent admissions have to be cancelled.” Ambulances were being diverted to other hospitals. As the bad news rolled in from each ward, Clayton sighed. “We have a couple of liver transplants to come in, no stroke capacity, six neuro patients on hold. It’s incredibly tight.” All wards in the hospital were on black, the highest alert level. Black has become the new normal; the second-highest level, red, is for good days. The day before, 15 non-urgent operations had been cancelled for lack of beds. Today, totting up the totals, Clayton found there were nine beds freed up by patients leaving, but 24 patients whose treatment could not be delayed were due to come in. Looking at the reports from each ward, Clayton calculated that up to 35 patients might leave the hospital that day, freeing up their beds, but she couldn’t be sure – they were waiting for test results and for final discharge from doctors. “Over the last year it’s got worse,” said Clayton. “This isn’t a crisis. This is how it is.” The population in the area around Addenbrooke’s is the fastest-growing in the country – and the elderly population is increasing even faster. Over the last four years, the number of over-85s Addenbrooke’s has treated has risen by 23%. On average, Clayton’s team needs to find 100 emergency beds every day, and that number keeps rising. Sometimes, demand can be much higher. On the Sunday before I visited, there were 309 admissions of patients who came in from emergency: these were very sick people, not lightly admitted in a hospital so severely pressed for beds. “We didn’t have a summer last year, no easing up. It’s winter all the time now,” said Clayton. She made it sound like Narnia, a frozen wasteland with no relief. Every service has targets to hit, waiting times and waiting lists to meet, but they all depend on one another. A week earlier, there had been 14 ambulances lined up outside the emergency department. They had to wait, caring for people inside their vans as they couldn’t offload patients for lack of cubicles and staff inside the hospital. When that happens, the ambulance service misses their target times for answering other calls, damaging their own inspection scores, knocking down the skittles from one service to another. Earlier in the week, the local radio news had even put out an urgent message to listeners asking them not to come to Addenbrooke’s emergency department unless their condition was severe. In response, fewer people turned up. But the bed shortage problem is not caused by too many frivolous cases, or by the many people who can’t get urgent GP appointments and therefore turn up at emergency. The problem is much bigger than that. * * * The NHS is bursting at the seams – hardly surprising after five years of the toughest budgets in its history. David Cameron kept his 2010 manifesto promise to increase NHS spending, but only by 0.8% a year – a homeopathic amount, the lowest in any parliament since the NHS began, even as the elderly population has grown each year. The UK spends less on health as a proportion of GDP than both the OECD and EU average. The UK has just 2.95 hospital beds per 1,000 people; Germany, by contrast, has 8.27 beds. The NHS is heading for a £2bn deficit this year, causing hospitals to run up debts to make up the shortfall. The government claims the NHS can and must make “efficiency savings” of £22bn by 2020 – a sum so unrealistic that few health economists outside the Department of Health believe it can be done. While the NHS struggles with its tightest-ever spending tourniquet, social care is close to being garotted Whenever the NHS financial crisis is raised, the government’s answer is for more people to be treated in the community, rather than in hospitals. This has been the dream of every health secretary for decades. The idea is that by improving everyday social care – the support the sick and frail get at home from social services – fewer patients end up needing to go to hospital and demand for beds will fall. But social care – funded by local councils – and the NHS are paid for from two separate budgets, both under the greatest financial stress they have ever faced. On average, half of council budgets are consumed by care for the frail; while the NHS struggles with its tightest-ever spending tourniquet, social care is close to being garotted. Local authorities say they lost 40% of their total funding in George Osborne’s first term as chancellor, and his summer budget threatens to take another 40% by 2020. This is a gigantic amount to cut from services already stripped to the bone. The Local Government Association – which represents all the councils in England, and is more Tory than Labour – this week sent its submission to the Treasury, warning that councils already have a £10bn black hole in their finances and can’t cope with more cuts. As funding disappears, the tension between the NHS and social care increases: each has an incentive to push patients on to the care of the other. A patient in hospital isn’t costing the council anything, while a patient moved quickly out of a hospital bed and sent home for social care relieves pressure on the NHS but puts pressure back on the council. Cambridgeshire is among many councils making heroic efforts to work closely with their local NHS, but social care has always been the poor relation of the mighty health service. Both sides are trying to shunt the ever-increasing bill for patients onto one another’s books. Related: 'Chronic underfunding' of social care increases burden on NHS, say GPs This push and pull between NHS and social care has led politicians to consider merging the two services under one budget, to remove any perverse incentives and ensure the money is spent jointly on the best and cheapest “patient pathway” in and out of hospital care. George Osborne is negotiating to devolve some parts of the NHS to local councils so they can run a joint service. Manchester, Cornwall and Sheffield are among his pilot schemes. But despite the flourish of a pre-election announcement, as yet no details have emerged of what exactly in the NHS will be devolved to councils, to whom or how. Above all, money remains the problem. Merging two cash-starved services will not result in new funding – and as the astute health commentator, Roy Lilley, asks, what makes anyone think putting together two turkeys makes a phoenix? * * * Straight after the morning bed state meeting at Addenbrooke’s comes the daily 9.30 staffing meeting, to see whether there are enough nurses and healthcare assistants on each ward. (Healthcare assistants do the basics – bed-making, washing, dressing, feeding and taking patients to the toilet.) A bed is not a bed unless it has enough staff to look after the patient in it. Like most hospitals, Addenbrooke’s has a huge bill for hiring extra nurses ad hoc from private agencies. The government cut training places for British nurses in 2010, and although more places have since been added, numbers are still lower than five years ago. British hospitals are frantically recruiting abroad, sending out agents to Ireland, Portugal, Spain and further afield, driving up costs as each hospital competes with the others to attract trained nurses. Addenbrooke’s recently recruited 100 Filipino nurses, but they will need three to six months of orientation – including English exams – before they can begin working. All the while, the demand for hospital beds increases. Who’s to blame for the overflowing beds at Addenbrooke’s? One possible culprit is an 85-year-old diabetic named Edward Morton. (Names of elderly patients have been changed.) He arrived at the hospital with a serious chest infection after a fall at home. When I spoke to Morton three weeks later, he couldn’t remember exactly how long he’d been at the hospital, but his treatment was finished. He’s a cheery man who talked of his long years as an agricultural worker living in a tied cottage until he was evicted on retirement. “Back in the day when I started out we worked with the horses, before the tractors,” he said. Every needless day that patients like Morton remain at Addenbrooke’s costs £650 On the day I met him, Morton was one of 53 patients occupying precious Addenbrooke’s beds who were ready to leave but could not be discharged – because they were waiting for NHS services such as community physiotherapy, or social care services such as home visits. In the jargon, these patients are DToCs – an acronym which makes them sound like recovering alcoholics, but actually stands for “delayed transfer of care”. These people are frail, mainly very old, many with dementia. They are judged as not needing acute medical treatment, but they do need community health and social care when they leave hospital. They wait and wait for a bed in a residential or nursing home, or for a package of care in their own home that includes hoists, grab rails, bath fittings and care assistant visits so they can manage alone. Hospital is no place for them: the longer they stay, the more confused and institutionalised they risk becoming, losing their ability to manage at home for themselves, needing higher levels of care when they do finally leave. Every needless day that patients like Morton remain at Addenbrooke’s costs £650 – £400 for the average cost of a bed and another £250 for the extra cost of delaying another patient who should be coming in, but instead will be sent to a private hospital or to another NHS hospital for their operation. This is financial madness, considering that the care Morton was waiting to have arranged in his own home would cost only £160 a week. In June, Age UK reported that between June 2010 and March 2015, 2.43m bed days were wasted, as people waited for social care so they could leave hospital. Edith Johnson, 99 years old, was another bed-blocker at Addenbrooke’s. She was cheerful, but exceedingly vague about just about everything. “Lovely nurses, lovely doctors, no complaint about anything!” she declared. She had had a chest infection and ulcerated legs but was ready to go. “I walked all down the corridor today!” she said. She spoke of her mother as if she were alive, and of her early life in a flour mill in south Wales. Johnson had a husband at home, also frail, and like Morton, she too was waiting for a package of care to be arranged by the social worker from the council. Until then, she was stuck – and so was the hospital. * * * Everyone understands the desperate need to get the bed-blockers out. Cambridgeshire county council and Addenbrooke’s work closely together to deal with this challenge. On several of the geriatric wards, the council has even installed social workers to search for the right care for each patient, so that they can leave as quickly as possible. But it is a struggle to find the few available and affordable places in care homes, or the private agencies that can provide carers to visit patients at home at the right time and for the right price in rural areas. And since Addenbrooke’s draws patients from north London to Lincolnshire, the hospital has to negotiate with a host of councils, some as efficient as Cambridgeshire but some unhelpfully dilatory in taking their DToCS back. Here’s the crunch, the point where the NHS and local council care services rub up against each other. To stop councils deliberately leaving patients with the NHS once someone has been declared fit to leave hospital, after 48 hours have passed, government rules require hospitals to charge that person’s council £150 for every extra day a bed is blocked (although that is still only a fraction of the £650 per day it really costs). Addenbrooke’s has decided to put those fines into a special fund that they give back to Cambridgeshire council to pay for social workers in the geriatric wards. Getting those patients out of the discharge lounge and through the exit every day is imperative. At Addenbrooke’s, a man whose healthcare assistant introduced him only as Roger was sitting in a wheelchair, wrapped in his coat. Roger had been fit enough to go for a long while but until now no nursing home place had opened up. “It’s a matter of dead men’s shoes,” one of the social workers later told me. A place only becomes available when a resident dies, so people too feeble to go back to their own homes must sit in an Addenbrooke’s bed waiting for some unknown death elsewhere. “You’re going to a home, you know that Roger? We’ve explained that to you,” his healthcare assistant said. “Am I? Well then, that’s where I’m going.” The porter waiting to wheel him out to transport that still hadn’t arrived said, “It’s a very nice home they’ve found for you, Roger,” though he had no way of knowing. “Oh, good,” Roger said, looking neither pleased nor alarmed, his dementia perhaps removing his capacity to register the difference between “home” and “a home”. The shortage of places means the private care companies, on whom all councils now rely, can set whatever price they wish According to Adrian Loades, the executive director for children, families and adults for Cambridgeshire, the problem is not simply the lack of care-home places – it is the cost. The shortage of places means the private care companies, on whom all councils now rely, can set whatever price they wish. “That’s what markets do,” Loades sighed. The average national cost of a place in a care home has increased by £723 in the past year, and now stands at £29,389 per year. But things are particularly bad in the east of England, where costs leapt by almost 10% in the past year, making it the most expensive region in the country. On average, a care-home place there costs £33,800. On the day we spoke, Loades was struggling to find a bed in a home for a 65-year-old with learning difficulties; the only quote he had was £1,400 a week, twice the price for existing residents they had already placed there. “They know they can charge what they like,” he said. The solution Loades proposes is remarkable: the council itself is considering re-opening its own care homes. The last one was sold off over 10 years ago, most of them to the private sector that is now charging the council these astronomical sums. Until the mid-1980s, councils ran their own residential and nursing homes and their own home-care services. However, Margaret Thatcher’s government forced councils to privatise most of the care sector by stopping the central social security system paying for anyone in a council home. If councils wanted to keep them open, they had to pay for the residents themselves. Budgets were then devolved from the Whitehall social security department to local councils – and most found the cost of running their own homes too high: as ever, state-run facilities are unionised and so have to pay reasonable wages, while the largely non-union private sector is free to cut costs, pay lower wages and often dispense with training. Many use zero-hours contracts to reduce staff costs yet further. Cambridgeshire, said Adrian Loades, held on until their homes needed extensive repairs they had no money to pay for. Now the market has the whip hand. “But,” he said, “we think we can open and run homes ourselves again at a lower cost than some private providers. We believe that in some cases this may encourage more expensive private homes to lower their fees.” He is now in discussions about buying the first home, a new facility. If Loades manages to implement his plan it would buck the trend towards increased privatisation – and it may demonstrate how state-run services can be cheaper than relying on soaring private prices. * * * Of course, most old people don’t want to go into residential care at all. The aim of social services, for financial and humane reasons, is to keep them in their own homes as long as possible – but care packages with frequent visits to frail people don’t come cheap. Councils buy these packages from thousands of private care agencies, some big, most small. One recipient of home care in Cambridgeshire is Sheila Lancaster. Ten years ago Sheila and her husband Eric moved to Cosy Nook, an upmarket caravan site in the countryside just north of Cambridge, where the wheels are hidden under brick walls, so the neat rows of mobile homes look like bungalows. It was “our dream retirement home,” Eric told me sadly. “Only it didn’t work out that way.” Sheila developed severe Alzheimer’s soon after moving in and now she needs three visits a day from two carers at a time to help lift her in and out of bed, to dress, wash and change incontinence pads. Before Sheila fell ill, Eric, a retired driving instructor, had never cooked, washed clothes or cleaned the house, but now he does it all – care assistants do not have time for those tasks on their flying visits. “I only buy food with cooking instructions on it – like a roasting chicken,” he told me. “I get by.” There is little chance that old people like these will get the council-financed meals-on-wheels service that used to be common, often delivered by volunteers bringing hot food and offering a little company to the isolated elderly every day. According to the Financial Times, 81% fewer people receive those meals than 10 years ago. Nor will most old people like the Lancasters be taken to day centres for company and care, as half of all day centres in England have closed since 2010, with many more under threat. The council pays for a care company, which received five ticks from the CQC’s last inspection two years ago, to visit Sheila Lancaster. Sheila’s care package started nearly a year ago after the district nurse visited. “I should have asked for help sooner if I’d known I could,” Eric said. (He had injured his back trying to lift Sheila alone.) At first there were half-hour visits, but as the council budget has shrunk, they have recently been cut back to 15-minute dashes. Eric grumbled, as most people do, about the rapid turnover of staff and the sometimes erratic hours they arrive, “and they’re always in a hurry”. Sometimes, he said, Sheila cries, afraid of new strangers undressing her. According to Eric’s records, Sheila has had 34 different carers in the last six months. “And many of them don’t speak English,” he said. But he is full of sympathy for them and has sometimes managed to talk to one or two. “Most wouldn’t say anything about their pay – but one, who was leaving, wrote it all down for me.” He pulled out the paper she had left. “She was on [a] zero hours [contract], and she reckoned she got less than £5 an hour after counting in her travel time and petrol, as it took her 25 minutes to get here. She worked 50 hours a week to earn £1000 a month before tax. No wonder they’re always in a rush.” On the day I visited, Sheila’s two carers didn’t arrive to get her up until after 11. They were fast and efficient, getting her out of bed, into the bathroom, washing her soiled body, getting her dressed and seated on the sitting room settee on a incontinence mat in all of 12 minutes, which left a few extra minutes to fill out the forms in their files. Of the estimated half-million home-care workers, only 20,000 belong to Unison, their union. They generally work alone, often on zero-hours contracts, afraid of getting less work if they complain. The last care minister, Norman Lamb, agreed that thousands of care workers, unpaid for their travel time, were not getting the legal minimum wage, and he helped implement a policy of naming and shaming under-paying employers. (Since 2013, 162 companies have been named and forced to pay financial penalties.) Cambridgeshire pays agencies £16 an hour, so wouldn’t it be cheaper for the council to run these services themselves too? “Probably not,” said Loades. That’s partly because the council would end up paying better wages and conditions – and they have no money. The new minimum wage of £9 per hour by 2020, announced in Osborne’s summer budget, will add another £1bn to their contracts with private care companies – but, Loades said: “There is no sign the government is going to pay us this extra.” Councils everywhere have had to tighten the criteria for how frail someone has to be before they are entitled to care; 150,000 fewer people are receiving care than in 2011, a 28% drop. In August, Andrea Sutcliffe, chief inspector of social care at the CQC, warned of the “stress and strain” in the system. In the first six months of the year, Sutcliffe has received 30,000 allegations of abuse of patients using social care services, double the number recorded four years ago. Underfunding, high staff turnover and low qualifications make social care a service in even greater crisis than the NHS. * * * When the NHS waves shrouds and sends up flares, politicians listen. When A&E wards overflow, when waiting times for operations soar, when new cancer drugs are denied or ambulances stack up outside hospitals for lack of beds, national headlines reverberate with fears for a service that has never suffered a period of such sustained austerity. Social care, meanwhile, suffers in silence. The public still registers satisfaction with council services, because most people only notice the state of the bins, the parks and the roads, with an occasional outcry about a library closure. Deep cuts to social care happen quietly, because although social care consumes on average half of councils’ entire annual budget, just 2% of the population use it at any one time. And as those people are by definition vulnerable, their voices are usually unheard or ignored. People like the Lancasters, Edward Morton and Edith Johnson are out of sight and out of mind. However, as the blocked DToC beds at Addenbrooke’s show, the two services are interdependent: when social care can’t cope, the NHS overflows. Would a full-scale merging of NHS and social care services help? Such reforms would require enormous administrative upheaval. The 2012 NHS re-organisation, costing £3bn, was described by the-then NHS England chief executive Sir David Nicholson as “so big you can see it from outer space”. It has left a depleted cadre of senior managers with neither the appetite nor capability to consider what health economist Prof Alan Maynard describes as yet another colossal “re-disorganisation”, shuffling the deck chairs when the overwhelming problem is money. One of the great obstacles to joining up the services is that NHS services are free, but anyone using social care, either in a residential home or for care visits to their own home, must pay for it themselves if they have savings over £23,250. In Cambridgeshire, for instance, about half the care provided is paid for by the council and half the bills are picked up by better-off patients. In a time when budgets are so squeezed, the blurred boundaries between the NHS and social care become battlelines. When, for instance, is a patient sick enough to fall under the care of the NHS (free) and when does their care count as social, falling upon local social services or the family? That’s a fuzzy distinction, as the NHS sends far sicker patients to convalesce at home than they used to. This causes major disputes over who’s responsible – not just between councils and the NHS, but between better-off families and the NHS. Take the case of Harold Jamieson and his family. Aged 85, he lives in a spacious house in Barrington, a picturesque village outside Cambridge. An ex-RAF test pilot, with an MBE and OBE, he has already fought and won a claim at a tribunal for war disability pension after a plane crash that damaged his back, shoulder and arm. He is now almost totally immobile, and suffers from a number of conditions, including peripheral neuropathy, swollen legs and pressure sores. Last year Jamieson and his family applied for the NHS continuing care fund to pick up his bills, a fund that pays for people’s care at home if they are sick enough to still count as an NHS responsibility. The Jamiesons applied for £20,000 a year for his 24-hour live-in carer from Lithuania – but they were rejected. Jamieson had to remortgage his house to pay for his care. The question at issue was whether his disability was severe enough to count as needing medical care (free from the NHS) or whether he just needed social care (to be paid for by Jamieson himself, as he had savings and property over the threshold of £23,250). The Jamiesons won their case on appeal last year, so Harold is certified as sick enough to qualify for NHS funding. It helped that his highly articulate daughter, Edwina Harris, is an ex-nurse who knew her way around this byzantine system, which she told me was a nightmare, even for her. Harris warned that very few people would know about the NHS continuing care fund, let alone how to request it and then to appeal. Patients face an impossible cat’s cradle of shrinking services and providers with criss-crossing responsibilities, no unified service, no simple pathway. As more people retire better-off than ever before, with more property and better pensions, it seems only right that they should contribute to their care. Yet this pay-as-you-go system leaves everyone’s fate subject to the lottery of life, where some will lose everything if they spend several years in a costly nursing home, while others who drop dead without needing care can pass everything on to their children. The grievous lack of money for any care for growing numbers of people who need it means that things are going to get worse. Here is a crisis that citizens need to confront, but all solutions require raising their taxes, or some new form of universal insurance, or a system for putting a lump sum into a pooled fund on retirement. However, the government’s “long-term plan” is to shrink, not to grow, services, reducing the size of the state to well below levels even Thatcher considered. The brunt will fall on the least publicly visible services, such as care of the old. The 53 bed-blockers of Addenbrooke’s are just one small emblem of an ever-growing army of marooned old people in need of good and kind care in their last declining years. Those without their own money will get fewer and shorter visits, die in shabbier nursing homes, staffed by underpaid and overworked people until the very word “care” becomes an affront to common decency. Squalor and misery in an uncared-for old age will be the fate of anyone who can’t pay for themselves – and many who can. • Follow the Long Read on Twitter at @gdnlongread, or sign up to the long read weekly email here.

people_mentioned.role: Author and journalist writing about the care crisis

time_periods.label: Period of NHS austerity and budget constraints under Conservative government

cross_domain_bridges: The healthcare crisis demonstrates economic principles of resource allocation and market failure, where separate budgets for NHS and social care create perverse incentives that increase overall costs. The demographic challenge of an aging population intersects with urban planning and social policy, as cities must redesign infrastructure and services for older residents. The article reveals how political ideology about public spending directly impacts medical outcomes, showing the intersection between fiscal policy and health science. The international recruitment of nurses from countries like the Philippines illustrates global labor migration patterns driven by policy decisions in wealthy nations.

film_adaptation.pitch: Following the daily crisis management at one of Britain's best hospitals as bed shortages force impossible decisions, revealing how austerity has turned healthcare into a zero-sum game between competing services.
